# AI Description

# Technical Description of Figure A-3.1.1.6.2

## 1. Diagram Type
This is a technical detail drawing showing measurement conventions for fenestration components. It consists of multiple cross-sectional and elevation views illustrating proper measurement techniques for various non-rectangular window and glazing configurations.

## 2. Building Components/Systems Shown
The diagram illustrates three distinct fenestration types:
- Bow window (top)
- Skylight (bottom left)
- Curved window (bottom right)

## 3. Visible Labels, Measurements, and Annotations

### Labels:
- "bow window" - identifying the top fenestration type
- "skylight" - identifying the bottom left fenestration type
- "curved window" - identifying the bottom right fenestration type
- "EG00733B" - drawing reference number (bottom right corner)

### Measurement Indicators:
- Double-headed arrows with dimension lines showing measurement extents
- Arrows pointing to specific measurement locations on each fenestration type
- Measurement lines extend to the outermost points of each glazing configuration

## 4. Relationships and Connections Between Elements

### Bow Window:
- Shows a multi-segmented angled window configuration
- Measurement arrows indicate that the area calculation should span the full width of the bow projection
- Multiple vertical support/frame elements are shown at segment junctions
- Horizontal measurement line spans the entire width from end to end

### Skylight:
- Depicts a triangular/peaked configuration typical of sloped roof installations
- Measurement arrows point to the base corners of the triangular opening
- Shows the frame/curb detail at connection points
- Indicates measurement should be taken at the widest points of the opening

### Curved Window:
- Illustrates a semi-circular or arched window configuration
- Measurement arrows indicate the span should be measured at the chord (straight-line distance)
- Shows frame mounting points at the base of the curve
- Demonstrates how to measure non-linear fenestration geometries

## 5. Compliance Requirements and Technical Specifications

### Context from Associated Text:
**Section A-3.1.1.6.(2) - Areas of Other Fenestration** states: "Figure A-3.1.1.6.(2) illustrates how to measure the area of glass panes as described in Sentence 3.1.1.6.(2)."

### Measurement Methodology:
- The diagram establishes the convention for measuring fenestration areas for code compliance calculations
- For non-rectangular fenestrations, measurements are taken at maximum extents
- The "rough opening" concept from Figure A-3.1.1.6.(1) is applied to these specialized configurations
- Measurements appear to be taken from frame edge to frame edge at the widest points

### Relationship to Energy Code Compliance:
- These measurement conventions are used to calculate fenestration area for thermal performance compliance
- Accurate area calculations are essential for determining overall building envelope performance
- This method differs from CSA A440.2/A440.3 standards, as noted in Section A-3.1.1.6.(1)
- The rough opening dimensions (rather than glass-only dimensions) are used to facilitate determination of compliance

### Application:
- This figure provides guidance for calculating areas of non-standard fenestration shapes
- Used in conjunction with thermal bridging calculations (Section A-3.1.1.5.(5)(a))
- Essential for determining compliance with energy performance requirements in Division B of NECB
- Applies to bow windows, skylights, curved windows, and similar non-rectangular fenestration products
